title: Your AI Agent Has Amnesia. You’re the Cure.
author: Christian Bourlier
text:
Your AI Agent Has Amnesia. You’re the Cure.
A 300-line task queue that gives Claude Code a memory between sessions.
Four Tools. One SQLite File.
dispatch is an MCP server that gives your AI agent a task queue. Four tools:
- create_task — add something to the queue
- get_tasks — list tasks by status
- claim_task — mark a task as in-progress
- complete_task — mark it done
That’s the entire API. Create, claim, complete. A task starts pending, moves to active when claimed, and ends at done. One state machine, no branching, no edge cases.
Everything lives in a single SQLite file at ~/.dispatch/tasks.db
. No daemon. No cloud. No network calls. Close the terminal, open it next week — your tasks are still there.
Why This Matters More Than It Looks
A task queue sounds simple. It is. That’s the point.
But what it enables isn’t simple. When your agent has persistent tasks, it can pick up work across sessions. “Check for pending tasks” becomes the first thing you say in a new session. The agent reads the queue, sees what’s outstanding, claims a task, and starts working. No re-explaining. No context dump. You went from a 5-minute ramp-up to a 5-second one.
The claim semantics matter too. claim_task
is transactional — SQLite ensures no two sessions grab the same work. Run multiple agents in parallel and they won't step on each other. Agent A claims the auth refactor. Agent B claims the test suite. No coordination meeting required.
What It Looks Like
Install it:
npm install -g @rezzed.ai/dispatch
Add to your MCP config:
{
"mcpServers": {
"dispatch": {
"command": "npx",
"args": ["@rezzed.ai/dispatch"]
}
}
}
Now tell your agent:
“Create a task to refactor the auth module, high priority”
create_task(title: "Refactor auth module", priority: "high")
→ Task created: abc-123
Next session, different day:
“What tasks are pending?”
get_tasks(status: "pending")
→ 1 task: "Refactor auth module" (high priority)
“Claim the auth task and start working”
claim_task(taskId: "abc-123")
→ Task claimed.
The agent picks up exactly where you left off. Or where a different agent left off. The queue doesn’t care who’s asking — it cares about state.
300 Lines. On Purpose.
The entire core logic is under 300 lines of TypeScript. That’s not a limitation — it’s a design constraint.
No authentication layer. No user management. No cloud sync. No telemetry. No config files. The storage is one SQLite table with nine columns. The API is four tools with two required parameters between them.
Every feature that doesn’t serve “create, claim, complete” got cut. Not deferred. Cut. A task queue doesn’t need webhook integrations. It doesn’t need a dashboard. It needs to work every time you open a terminal and never make you think about it.
SQLite handles the hard parts — atomic transactions, concurrent access, crash recovery. dispatch handles the simple parts — four MCP endpoints that map directly to the task lifecycle. The complexity budget went to zero because the dependency did the heavy lifting.
The Quiet Multiplier
dispatch isn’t flashy. It doesn’t generate code or review PRs or write tests. It does one thing: it remembers what you were doing.
That turns out to be the thing that makes everything else work. An agent that can pick up tasks across sessions compounds. Monday’s agent creates three tasks. Tuesday’s agent finishes two and creates one more. Wednesday’s agent clears the queue and starts the next feature.
No re-explaining. No lost context. No “wait, where were we?” Just a queue that persists and an agent that reads it.
Your AI agent is fast. But it’s amnesic. dispatch is the fix.
Built by Rezzed — open source AI developer tools.
